location.origin: Seattle, Washington (born September 7, 1942). Son of a minister. Raised in Seattle's Central District. Learned piano on grandmother's instrument. Began saxophone at age ten. Attended Garfield High School -- the same school as Quincy Jones (degree 0) and Jimi Hendrix.
location.formative: Seattle Central District (1950s-1960s). Played saxophone in The Velvetones -- Jimi Hendrix's first band -- at teen dances at Yesler Terrace. Passed through The Stags, The Nite Sounds, The Emergency Exit. Switched from saxophone to bass guitar. Co-founded Ballin' Jack (1969) with Ronnie Hammon, both Garfield High School friends of Hendrix.
location.career: Ballin' Jack (1969-1974): Columbia Records debut (1970, No. 180 Billboard), 'Super Highway' (No. 93 Hot 100). Toured with Jimi Hendrix on 1970 Cry of Love tour, opening at Fillmore East and West. Relocated to Los Angeles. Two further albums on Mercury Records (Special Pride 1973, Live And In Color 1974). Band dissolved 1974. Santana (1976): Lead vocalist on European tour (Amigos Tour), replacing Greg Walker. War (1979-c.1984): Replaced B.B. Dickerson on bass. Albums: The Music Band 2 (MCA, 1979), Outlaw (RCA, 1982), Life (Is So Strange) (RCA, 1983). Replaced by Ricky Green c.1984. Car accident 1986 caused neck injury and hand numbness, ending bass career. Transitioned to management/production. Stroke 2002 caused chest-down paralysis. Died January 22, 2006, in Los Angeles, age 63. Buried at Forest Lawn Memorial Park (Hollywood Hills).

labels: Columbia Records (1970-1972, Ballin' Jack -- two albums)
Mercury Records (1973-1974, Ballin' Jack -- two albums)
MCA Records (1979, War -- The Music Band 2)
RCA Records (1982-1983, War -- Outlaw and Life (Is So Strange))

associated_musicians.name: Jimi Hendrix
associated_musicians.role: Bandmate (The Velvetones, late 1950s), tour support (Ballin' Jack opened for Hendrix on 1970 Cry of Love tour), Garfield High School classmate
associated_musicians.note: Born November 27, 1942, Seattle -- same year as Rabb. Both raised in the Central District. Both attended Garfield High School. The Velvetones were Hendrix's first band; Rabb played tenor and baritone saxophone. Ballin' Jack toured with Hendrix at the Fillmore East and West. Hendrix encouraged Ballin' Jack's signing to Columbia Records. Hendrix died September 18, 1970. The strongest engine-to-Hendrix documented link.

associated_musicians.name: Ronnie Hammon
associated_musicians.role: Co-founder of Ballin' Jack, Garfield High School classmate, lifelong musical partner
associated_musicians.note: Hammon (drums) and Rabb (bass/vocals) co-founded Ballin' Jack in Seattle in 1969. Both had attended Garfield High School with Hendrix.

associated_musicians.name: Carlos Santana
associated_musicians.note: Rabb replaced Greg Walker as Santana's lead vocalist for the 1976 Amigos European tour. His vocals were reportedly overdubbed by Walker for the Moonflower live album (1977). [NEEDS VERIFICATION -- overdub details.]

associated_musicians.name: Lonnie Jordan
associated_musicians.note: Jordan is War's only continuous original member. He and Rabb played together from 1979 to c.1984 during War's MCA/RCA transition period.

associated_musicians.name: Howard Scott
associated_musicians.note: Scott co-founded War with Harold Brown. African American guitarist from South Central LA. His guitar work alongside Rabb's bass defined War's groove in the early 1980s.

associated_musicians.name: Lee Oskar
associated_musicians.note: Born in Copenhagen, Denmark. Oskar's harmonica was War's most distinctive timbral signature. His European melodic sensibility combined with Rabb's Seattle-trained bass to create a transatlantic groove fusion.

associated_musicians.name: Harold Brown
associated_musicians.note: Co-founder of War with Howard Scott. Brown's drumming locked with Rabb's bass to form War's rhythm section during Rabb's tenure.

associated_musicians.name: B.B. Dickerson
associated_musicians.note: Morris Dewayne Dickerson, born August 3, 1949, Torrance, California. The original War bassist who created the groove vocabulary ('Low Rider' bass line, 'Cisco Kid' bass line) that Rabb inherited and continued. Died April 18, 2021.

associated_musicians.name: Charles Miller
associated_musicians.note: Born June 2, 1939, Olathe, Kansas. Killed in a robbery June 14, 1980 -- during Rabb's tenure with the band. Miller's sax/flute work was integral to War's horn-driven sound.

associated_musicians.name: Papa Dee Allen
associated_musicians.note: Born July 19, 1931, Wilmington, Delaware. Died of a brain aneurysm on stage August 30, 1988, at the Pavilion in San Jose, California. Allen's congas were the Latin-percussion backbone of War's multicultural sound.

associated_musicians.name: Eric Burdon
associated_musicians.note: Former lead singer of the British Invasion band The Animals. Burdon fronted Eric Burdon and War for their first two albums before departing due to exhaustion and management disputes. 'Spill the Wine' (1970, No. 3 Hot 100) was the Burdon-era hit.

associated_musicians.name: Jerry Goldstein
associated_musicians.note: Goldstein conceived the Eric Burdon and War collaboration after seeing the musicians backing Deacon Jones at the Rag Doll club in North Hollywood (1969). He produced and managed War throughout their career.

theory_nodes.concept: The Foundation (artist-city pattern where an artist is born and raised in the city, formed entirely by its musical culture, and then carries that formation outward as the structural base of larger musical enterprises -- Rabb was born in Seattle, raised in the Central District, educated at Garfield High School, formed in the Velvetones and the band scene, then carried Seattle's musical culture as the bass/groove foundation of Ballin' Jack, Santana, and War; the bass guitar IS the musical foundation; the pattern name reflects the instrument)
examples: Luther Rabb (degree 32) -- born Seattle, Garfield High School, Velvetones with Hendrix, Ballin' Jack co-founder, Santana vocalist, War bassist. In each case, Rabb was the structural base -- the bass, the groove, the foundation -- not the leader.

Contrast with Export (degree 0, Quincy Jones) -- Jones left Seattle and became a leader (producer, arranger, composer). Rabb left Seattle and became a foundation (bassist, groove provider, structural support).

Contrast with The Relay (degree 31, Brenda Lee Eager) -- Eager never lived in Seattle but carried its traditions through musical collaboration. Rabb was BORN in Seattle and carried its traditions physically.
Contrast with The Incubation (degree 30, Ray Charles) -- Charles was incubated by the city and transformed the world. Rabb was formed by the city and supported others' transformations.
theory_nodes.description: The Foundation is the thirty-third artist-city pattern in the Seattle 360 engine. It describes an artist who is born and raised in the city, formed entirely by its musical culture, and then carries that formation outward to serve as the structural base of larger musical enterprises. Luther Rabb was born in Seattle, raised in the Central District, attended Garfield High School (the same school as Quincy Jones, degree 0), played in the Velvetones (Jimi Hendrix's first band), and co-founded Ballin' Jack before carrying his Seattle-trained musicianship to Santana and War. In each context, Rabb was not the leader but the foundation -- the bass player, the groove anchor, the structural element that the music rested on. The pattern name reflects the instrument: the bass guitar IS the musical foundation. Rabb's career is the engine's most direct expression of the city-as-foundation principle: Seattle made the musician, the musician became the foundation of other musicians' enterprises.

ancestors: Central District musical culture (Jackson Street clubs, Garfield High School, Yesler Terrace teen dances) -- the ecosystem that formed Rabb's musicianship
The Velvetones (Hendrix's first band) -- the specific origin point of Rabb's musical career
Garfield High School (shared with Jones degree 0 and Hendrix) -- the institutional node in the Central District's musical network
Ballin' Jack (Seattle horn-rock) -- the first enterprise where Rabb served as bass foundation
Ray Charles (degree 30) Central District incubation -- the previous generation's absorption of the same neighborhood's musical culture
genealogy.relationship: The Foundation pattern exists because a city's musical culture does not produce only leaders and innovators. It also produces the foundation players -- the bassists, the rhythm section members, the structural supports without whom the leaders cannot lead. Rabb is the engine's first documented foundation player: a musician of exceptional skill whose career was defined by supporting other musicians' visions rather than imposing his own. The Foundation is not lesser than the Export -- it is complementary. Jones (degree 0) could not have been Jones without the Central District; Rabb could not have been Rabb without the same Central District. The city produced both the leader and the foundation.

theory_nodes.concept: The groove lock (thirty-third acoustic element -- the moment when a bass line locks into a repeating, hypnotic groove pattern that becomes the immovable foundation on which every other instrument builds; named for War's signature sound; 'Low Rider' IS a groove lock; the bass does not vary, does not yield; it holds the entire ensemble in place through gravitational force; follows the mellow undertow (degree 31) -- after the warm sub-surface current, the declarative groove foundation)
examples: Luther Rabb (degree 32) -- the groove lock is War's signature: 'Low Rider,' 'Slippin' Into Darkness,' 'The Cisco Kid' are all groove locks. The bass line initiates a pattern so compelling that the entire band and audience surrender to it.

Brenda Lee Eager (degree 31) -- 'the mellow undertow': warm, sub-surface, pulling. The groove lock is the opposite -- declarative, structural, on the surface. The undertow operates beneath; the groove lock IS the surface.

Foxy Apollo (degree 22) -- 'resonant percussion / the groove reset': the groove reset interrupts and re-establishes. The groove lock establishes and does NOT release. The lock is permanent; the reset is intermittent.
Polyrhythmics (degree 21) -- 'the hammer': percussive impact. The groove lock is sustained impact -- not one strike but continuous gravitational hold.
theory_nodes.description: The groove lock is the thirty-third acoustic element in the Seattle 360 engine. It is named for the defining acoustic event of War's music and of Luther Rabb's bass playing: the moment when a bass line locks into a repeating, hypnotic groove pattern that becomes the immovable foundation on which every other instrument builds. The groove lock is not a riff -- it is a rhythmic-harmonic event that, once initiated, holds the entire ensemble in place through gravitational force. 'Low Rider' is the paradigmatic groove lock: that bass line, once it begins, does not stop, does not vary, does not yield. It is a foundation that other instruments orbit. The groove lock follows the mellow undertow (degree 31) in the acoustic progression. The undertow was warm, subtle, pulling from beneath the surface. The groove lock is declarative, structural, and on the surface. The undertow pulls; the groove lock holds. After the post-brrit warmth phase, the groove lock asserts a new acoustic phase: the rhythmic foundation as the primary acoustic event.

ancestors: The mellow undertow (degree 31, Brenda Lee Eager) -- warm sub-surface current; the groove lock is the surface declaration that follows the sub-surface warmth
The rough brrit (degree 30, Ray Charles) -- the eruption that broke the liquid triad; the groove lock is the new stability after the eruption and its undertow recovery
Resonant percussion / the groove reset (degree 22, Foxy Apollo) -- intermittent groove re-establishment; the groove lock is permanent groove establishment
The hammer (degree 21, Polyrhythmics) -- percussive impact; the groove lock is sustained impact
Ostinato (degree 13, Marc Seales) -- repeating melodic figure; the groove lock is the rhythmic/harmonic version of the ostinato, applied specifically to the bass guitar's groove function
genealogy.relationship: Thirty-three acoustic elements across thirty-three degrees. The groove lock opens a new phase defined by rhythmic foundation as primary acoustic event. The progression mellow undertow (31) -> groove lock (32) maps the transition from sub-surface warmth to surface-level rhythmic architecture. The post-brrit sequence is now: recovery (undertow) -> declaration (groove lock). The bass as foundation, the groove as architecture, the lock as permanence.

theory_nodes.concept: Genre refusal at band scale (the phenomenon where a band's music resists every genre category because the multiracial, multiethnic composition of the band produces a sound that IS itself rather than a blend OF other genres; War's funk-soul-rock-jazz-Latin fusion is not eclecticism but the organic product of musicians from different cultural backgrounds playing their shared neighborhoods' music; 'Low Rider' is not 'funk with Latin elements' -- it is Low Rider music)
examples: War (degree 32) -- seven-piece multiracial band (African American, Mexican American, Danish). Funk, soul, rock, jazz, Latin, R&B coexist without hierarchy. The genre IS the band.
Contrast with Reggie Watts (degree 17, The Uncategorizable) -- individual genre refusal through comedic-musical improvisation. War is collective genre refusal through multicultural band composition.
Contrast with Black Stax (degree 25, Neo-Soul/Hip-Hop) -- genre blending with identifiable components. War's components are no longer identifiable as separate genres.
The CSV designates War's genre as 'R&B' -- the market category. But the actual music transcends R&B. The gap between market label and sonic reality is the genre refusal itself.
theory_nodes.description: Genre refusal at band scale is a structural concept documenting the phenomenon where a multiracial, multiethnic band produces music that resists all genre categories. War is the paradigmatic case: a seven-piece band with African American, Mexican American, and Danish members, drawing on funk, soul, rock, jazz, Latin, and R&B traditions simultaneously. The result is not a blend OF genres -- it is a sound that makes genre boundaries irrelevant. 'Low Rider' is not 'funk with Latin elements.' It is Low Rider music. The category IS the song. This is distinct from individual genre refusal (Reggie Watts, degree 17) because it is produced by the collective -- the genre refusal emerges from the interaction of different cultural backgrounds within a single band, not from one person's deliberate boundary-crossing. War's genre refusal is organic rather than intentional. The musicians played the music of their shared neighborhoods; the neighborhoods happened to be ethnically diverse; the music that emerged could not be contained by any single genre label.

ancestors: The Uncategorizable (degree 17, Reggie Watts) -- individual genre refusal. War extends this to the collective level.
The Workshop (degree 18, Maktub) -- the band as collaborative space where genres interact. War takes this further: the genres don't interact; they cease to exist as separate entities.
Multicultural LA neighborhoods (Compton, South Central, East LA) -- the social geography that produced War's sound
Eric Burdon's British blues-rock tradition -- the external catalyst that crystallized War's multiracial lineup into a recording act
genealogy.relationship: Genre refusal at band scale is the collective extension of individual genre refusal. Where Watts (degree 17) refuses genre through personal artistic choice, War refuses genre through collective cultural composition. The refusal is not a decision but a condition: when seven musicians from different cultural backgrounds play together, the music they make cannot be reduced to any one of their individual traditions. The genre IS the band.

theory_nodes.concept: Blue note -- funk bass bend (thirty-second deployment; the blue note expressed through the bass guitar's rhythmic and tonal vocabulary -- the moment when a bass line bends a note slightly flat, slightly behind the beat, or slightly outside the chord, creating the micro-tension that transforms a rhythmic pattern into a groove; the ingredient that makes 'Low Rider' feel alive rather than mechanical; the bend that makes you move)
examples: Luther Rabb (degree 32) -- the funk bass bend in War's music: every War bass line contains micro-bends that make the groove feel organic. The bass does not land exactly on the beat; it lands slightly behind, creating rhythmic tension the listener feels physically.
Brenda Lee Eager (degree 31) -- duet vocal warmth: relational blue note between two voices. The funk bass bend is structural -- within a single instrument's relationship to the beat.
Quincy Jones (degree 0) -- bass-line blue note: the original bass deployment. Degree 32 returns to the bass as blue note vehicle, completing a 32-degree bass arc.
Ivan 'Boogaloo Joe' Jones (degree 29) -- boogaloo guitar bend: the guitar's groove micro-tension. The funk bass bend is the bass's equivalent.
theory_nodes.description: Thirty-second deployment of the blue note thread. The funk bass bend is the blue note expressed through the bass guitar's rhythmic and tonal vocabulary: the moment when a bass line bends a note slightly flat, slightly behind the beat, or slightly outside the chord, creating the micro-tension that transforms a rhythmic pattern into a groove. In War's music, the funk bass bend is ubiquitous. Every War bass line -- from 'Low Rider' to 'Slippin' Into Darkness' to 'The Cisco Kid' -- contains micro-bends that make the groove feel organic rather than precise. The bass does not land exactly on the beat; it lands slightly behind, slightly into it, creating a rhythmic tension that the listener feels in their body before they hear it in their ears. This is the blue note as physical sensation -- the bend that makes you move. The funk bass bend also completes a bass arc that began at degree 0 (Quincy Jones, bass-line blue note). Thirty-two degrees later, the blue note returns to the bass -- this time as a funk groove mechanism rather than a jazz bass-line phenomenon.

ancestors: Blue note -- all thirty-one prior deployments (degrees 0-31)
Blue note -- bass-line (degree 0, Quincy Jones); the original bass deployment. Degree 32 returns to bass, completing a 32-degree arc.
Blue note -- duet vocal warmth (degree 31, Brenda Lee Eager); relational. The funk bass bend is structural.
Blue note -- boogaloo guitar bend (degree 29, Ivan 'Boogaloo Joe' Jones); guitar groove micro-tension. The funk bass bend is the bass equivalent.
Blue note -- bass fundamental (degree 8, Gary Peacock); jazz bass precision. The funk bass bend is funk bass groove -- imprecision as the mechanism.
genealogy.relationship: Thirty-two deployments across thirty-three degrees. The funk bass bend returns the blue note to the bass guitar after a 32-degree journey. The bass-line blue note at degree 0 was jazz -- precise, intentional, melodically driven. The funk bass bend at degree 32 is funk -- imprecise, groove-driven, rhythmically felt. The 32-degree arc maps the transformation of the bass's blue note from jazz precision to funk groove. Thirty-two mechanisms: bass-line (0), guitar (1), vocal (3), trumpet-warm (4), piano crush (5), altered voicings (6), trombone slide (7), bass fundamental (8), sax voice (9), alto cool bend (10), flute bent (11), bebop chromatic (12), modal integration (13), post-genre floating (14), assertive brass (15), synthesizer pitch bend (16), vocal glide (17), soul-vocal (18), collective-soul (19), americana-soul (20), funk-brass (21), vocal-funk (22), gospel-vocal (23), dynastic-soul (24), collective-activist (25), metamorphic (26), brass-section smear (27), supper-club vocal bend (28), boogaloo guitar bend (29), gospel-soul rasp (30), duet vocal warmth (31), funk bass bend (32).

seattle_connection: NATIVE SON -- born in Seattle (September 7, 1942), raised in the Central District, son of a minister, attended Garfield High School (same school as Quincy Jones degree 0 and Jimi Hendrix). Played saxophone in The Velvetones -- Jimi Hendrix's first band -- at teen dances at Yesler Terrace. Co-founded Ballin' Jack in Seattle (1969) with Garfield classmate Ronnie Hammon. Strongest biographical Seattle connection since degree 0 (Quincy Jones). THE FOUNDATION as 33rd artist-city pattern -- born in the city, formed by the city, carrying the city's musical culture as the structural base of larger enterprises. THE GROOVE LOCK as 33rd acoustic element -- the bass line locks into hypnotic pattern that holds the ensemble. R&B as Genre Stage 14 -- pure R&B, single word, first standalone. E=5 BREAKS THE E=4 STREAK -- first E=5 in the engine, ending eleven consecutive E=4 (degrees 21-31). Distribution 2:13:6:11:1. MERCURY RECORDS second consecutive degree (31-32). Label streak extends: Prestige-Atlantic-Mercury-Mercury (29-30-31-32). GARFIELD HIGH SCHOOL closes 32-degree arc: degree 0 and degree 32 shared the same school. HENDRIX CONNECTION strongest in the engine: Rabb was in Hendrix's first band (Velvetones), toured with Hendrix (Cry of Love tour). WAR: best-selling album of 1973 (The World Is a Ghetto), 'Low Rider' No. 1 R&B, 'The Cisco Kid' No. 2 Hot 100. BLUE NOTE: thirty-second deployment (funk bass bend -- micro-tension that transforms pattern into groove). GENDER: 6:27 ratio (~18.2%). Male. No change in women count.

The groove lock (thirty-third acoustic element; bass line locks into repeating hypnotic pattern; the immovable foundation; War's 'Low Rider' as paradigmatic groove lock)
Genre refusal at band scale (multiracial band produces music that resists all genre categories; War's seven-piece multicultural lineup; the genre IS the band)

engine_sequencing: E=5 BREAKS THE STREAK: First E=5 at degree 32. Eleven consecutive E=4 (21-31) ended. Distribution 2:13:6:11:1. Track whether E=5 recurs or engine returns to lower energy.
GENRE Stage 14 at degree 32: Pure R&B. Single word. First standalone R&B in the engine. Track at degree 33.
GROOVE LOCK opens new acoustic phase: rhythmic foundation as primary event. Post-undertow declaration. Track element 34 at degree 33.
MERCURY RECORDS second consecutive degree (31-32). Four-degree label streak: Prestige-Atlantic-Mercury-Mercury (29-30-31-32). Track.
GARFIELD HIGH SCHOOL arc: degrees 0 and 32. 32-degree institutional closure. Track if other Garfield alumni appear.
HENDRIX THREAD: Rabb was in Hendrix's first band. Strongest engine-to-Hendrix link. Track.

cultural_patterns: Artist-city taxonomy: THIRTY-THREE named patterns (adding The Foundation at degree 32). Classify every degree.
Gender ratio: 6:27 (women:men/male-led). Rabb = male. No change in women count. ~18.2%. Track.
HENDRIX CONNECTION thread: Velvetones (Hendrix's first band), Cry of Love tour, Garfield HS. Track.
WAR CULTURAL LEGACY: 'Low Rider' as Chicano cultural anthem. Music as community infrastructure. Track.
MULTIRACIAL BAND COMPOSITION: War's African American, Mexican American, Danish lineup. Genre refusal through cultural diversity. Track.
BASS AS FOUNDATION: Rabb's career defined by the structural support role. The foundation player as career archetype. Track.
